ontology.foodex2_description: Barley tea (mugicha) — roasted barley infusion, ready-to-drink, no sugar added
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers grain-based infusion beverages; applied as closest defensible match for packaged unsweetened barley tea, which is a specific type of grain-based infusion.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged barley tea beverage; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard packaged bottle of barley tea (approximately 350 ml / 350 g) is a typical single-serve portion consumed as a beverage with meals or as a refreshment.

nutrient_source.title: Barley Tea (No Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database entry for packaged unsweetened barley tea (no sugar added) used as primary source. All per-100g values sourced directly from this entry. Sodium reported as 3 mg/100g; all other macros confirmed zero by HPB.

gi_source.title: N/A — Barley Tea (No Sugar, Packaged) contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains 0 g carbohydrates per 100g per HPB Foodo. No GI value exists or is meaningful. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no entry for zero-carbohydrate barley tea. glycemic_index set to numeric 0 to satisfy template numeric requirement; gi_category is 'not_applicable' to correctly convey that GI assessment is not relevant for this zero-carbohydrate beverage.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100 g); zero sugar; no vasopressor nutrients
hypertension.guidance: With only 3 mg sodium per 100 g and zero calories, unsweetened packaged barley tea is a suitable daily beverage for individuals managing hypertension and can be consumed freely within normal fluid intake guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened packaged barley tea contains 0 g carbohydrates and 0 g sugar per 100 g, producing no postprandial glucose response, making it an appropriate calorie-free beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, negligible sodium; polyphenol content from roasted barley
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened barley tea contains no fat, no saturated fat, and only 3 mg sodium per 100 g; roasted barley polyphenols (including alkylresorcinols and chlorogenic acid derivatives) may confer modest cardioprotective antioxidant effects, supporting its use as a heart-friendly beverage.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenol and antioxidant content from roasted barley; negligible sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsweetened barley tea is neutral to mildly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis due to its polyphenol content; however, it does not provide sufficient omega-3 fatty acids, high-dose antioxidants, or anti-inflammatory nutrients to serve as a primary dietary intervention for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barley Tea (No Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink roasted barley infusion sold in sealed bottles or cartons without any added sugar or sweeteners. It is produced by steeping or extracting roasted barley grains in water, yielding a light amber to golden-brown beverage with a mild, toasty, slightly nutty flavour and a clean, refreshing finish. The drink is calorie-free, containing 0 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrates per 100 g, with only 3 mg sodium per 100 g. It is widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia — particularly in China, Japan (as mugicha), Korea (as boricha), and Singapore — as a daily hydration beverage served hot or cold. In Singapore, packaged unsweetened barley tea is a popular convenience-store and supermarket product consumed at breakfast, with meals, or as a between-meal refreshment. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (no glycaemic impact), hypertension (negligible sodium), and cardiovascular disease (zero saturated fat, polyphenol content). Roasted barley contains polyphenolic compounds including alkylresorcinols and chlorogenic acid derivatives that may provide modest antioxidant and vascular benefits. The beverage is vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, though it is not gluten-free due to its barley origin.
common_user_queries: Is barley tea good for diabetes?
Is barley tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The food is a standardised packaged product with consistent formulation. All per-100g values are confirmed by HPB with confidence 0.95. Source match type is exact.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — barley tea is an aqueous infusion with no protein retained in the liquid phase at commercial production scale. fat_g is naturally zero — no fat is present in the brewed barley infusion. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry for this packaged unsweetened product; trace soluble compounds are below detection threshold. energy_kcal is naturally zero as a consequence of zero protein, fat, and carbohydrate. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for packaged unsweetened barley tea are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product, and no supplementary data was available from USDA FoodData Central for an exact match. Glycemic index: this food contains 0 g carbohydrates per 100 g; GI assessment is not applicable. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 (the only carbohydrate-free value consistent with template numeric type requirement) and gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to correctly convey non-applicability. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no entry exists for zero-carbohydrate barley tea. Allergen note: barley contains gluten; this product is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity despite being a liquid infusion. FoodEx2 code A0FKP applied as closest defensible analogous match for grain-based infusion beverages.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name present. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names provided, satisfying Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirements. Food identity is precise and unambiguous.

Zero-macro justification documented in notes field using 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' phrases as required by g4 rule.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (3.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), and sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values supported by HPB Foodo source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 (satisfying the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric). gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to correctly convey that GI assessment is not relevant for this zero-carbohydrate beverage. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database consulted and rationale for non-applicability stated. The notes field clarifies the distinction between the numeric placeholder value and the clinical non-applicability of GI for this food.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for barley tea (FOODON_03301103). FoodEx2 code A0FKP applied as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT left null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters (approximately 1,350 characters). Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and nutritional profile. Suitable for RAG and LLM training use.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input data.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 12 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese (mugicha), and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for all relevant languages. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

Updated gi_source notes and record notes to clarify that numeric 0 is a template-compliant placeholder and gi_category 'not_applicable' conveys the clinical non-applicability of GI for this zero-carbohydrate food.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match, confidence 0.95). All core macros are confirmed zero with documented justification. Glycemic index set to numeric 0 (template-compliant) with gi_category 'not_applicable' to correctly represent that GI assessment is not clinically relevant for this zero-carbohydrate beverage. Multilingual naming satisfies Singaporean cuisine requirements (English, Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description is substantive and RAG-ready. Ontology is honest with no invented codes.
